summary:
Brachytherapy delivers irradiation through direct implant placement near cancerous areas using various forms such as seeds, wires, etc., making it a form of internal radiotherapy distinct from external beam techniques.
The patient receives high-dose rate treatments delivered precisely where needed due to its targeted nature compared to other methods which may affect larger volumes outside target sites more broadly.
Brachytherapy provides precise localization allowing higher doses at tumor locations while minimizing exposure elsewhere throughout the body during treatment sessions when contrasted against conventional approaches utilizing distant source applications via linear accelerators or cobalt machines among others not involving physical insertion mechanisms present here. To summarize concisely focusing only on critical aspects related to your query about what makes up 'irradiating' processes used clinically today –
It primarily refers to inserting small amounts of radionuclides right next-to-or-into cancers thereby concentrating effects locally without needing bulky equipment typically associated externally directed therapies; thus fitting well described criteria outlined above regarding specific type utilized versus general categories encompassing broader definitions potentially applicable across multiple modalities beyond just those employing intracorporeal placements alone but still falling generally speaking under umbrella term ‘radiotherapeutic’ interventions overall regardless their exact implementation details varying widely depending upon individual case needs being addressed therapeutically amongst many possible options available nowadays ranging far wider scope than mere
